It often begins with who, which, that, where, when or whose.

The main sentence still works without them.
The correct sentence is: My cousin (the youngest in the family) won the chess trophy.

nearMiss: My cousin the youngest in the family won the chess trophy.

_answerSpec.minimalHint: Parenthesis adds extra information that could be lifted out.

items.promptText: Why does the hyphen matter in small-animal hospital rather than small animal hospital?
_solutionLines: A hyphen can join words so they work as one describing idea before a noun.
The hyphen joins 'small' and 'animal' so they work together before 'hospital'.

golden: The hyphen shows that the hospital is for small animals.
nearMiss: The hyphen shows that the hospital building is small.
The hyphen marks a fronted adverbial.
The hyphen shows a missing letter.
_answerSpec.feedbackLong: The hyphen joins 'small' and 'animal' so they work together before 'hospital'.
_answerSpec.answerText: The hyphen shows that the hospital is for small animals.
_answerSpec.minimalHint: Say the phrase both ways in your head. Which version makes the meaning clear?

items.promptText: Why does the hyphen matter in last-minute poster rather than last minute poster?
_solutionLines: A hyphen can join words so they work as one describing idea before a noun.
The compound adjective comes before the noun, so the hyphen protects the intended meaning.

golden: The hyphen shows that 'last-minute' is one describing idea before 'poster'.
nearMiss: The hyphen shows that the poster is the final minute.
The hyphen joins two complete clauses.
The hyphen marks direct speech.
_answerSpec.feedbackLong: The compound adjective comes before the noun, so the hyphen protects the intended meaning.
_answerSpec.answerText: The hyphen shows that 'last-minute' is one describing idea before 'poster'.
_answerSpec.minimalHint: Say the phrase both ways in your head. Which version makes the meaning clear?

items.promptText: Why is this sentence a question? Where did the caretaker leave the keys?

items.promptText: Which option correctly punctuates the direct speech AND keeps the whole sentence as a question? Did Mum really say ___

_answerSpec.minimalHint: Check where the spoken words end and where the reporting clause begins.

items.promptText: Which option correctly joins a fronted adverbial to a main clause AND uses the right boundary punctuation? before the bell rang / the children lined up quietly
_solutionLines: This question tests adverbials and clauses and boundary punctuation together.
A fronted adverbial clause needs a comma after it to mark the boundary between the adverbial and the main clause. A semicolon is wrong because the adverbial is not an independent clause.
The correct option is: Before the bell rang, the children lined up quietly.

golden: Before the bell rang, the children lined up quietly.
nearMiss: Before the bell rang the children lined up quietly.
Before, the bell rang the children lined up quietly.
Before the bell rang; the children lined up quietly.
_answerSpec.feedbackLong: A fronted adverbial clause needs a comma after it to mark the boundary between the adverbial and the main clause. A semicolon is wrong because the adverbial is not an independent clause.
_answerSpec.answerText: Before the bell rang, the children lined up quietly.
_answerSpec.minimalHint: If the sentence opens with when, where or how information, check whether it needs a comma after it.

items.promptText: Which option correctly punctuates the sentence with a fronted adverbial AND identifies the main clause boundary? after the storm cleared / we inspected the damage
_solutionLines: This question tests adverbials and clauses and boundary punctuation together.
The adverbial 'After the storm cleared' needs a comma to show where the subordinate clause ends and the main clause begins. A colon is not used to join an adverbial to a main clause.
The correct option is: After the storm cleared, we inspected the damage.

golden: After the storm cleared, we inspected the damage.
nearMiss: After the storm cleared we inspected the damage.
After the storm, cleared we inspected the damage.
After the storm cleared: we inspected the damage.
_answerSpec.feedbackLong: The adverbial 'After the storm cleared' needs a comma to show where the subordinate clause ends and the main clause begins. A colon is not used to join an adverbial to a main clause.
_answerSpec.answerText: After the storm cleared, we inspected the damage.
_answerSpec.minimalHint: If the sentence opens with when, where or how information, check whether it needs a comma after it.

items.promptText: Which option places the comma correctly when the adverbial clause appears at the front? although the path was icy / nobody slipped
_solutionLines: This question tests adverbials and clauses and boundary punctuation together.
'Although the path was icy' is a subordinate adverbial clause showing concession. It needs a comma after it because it is fronted. A semicolon would wrongly treat it as an independent clause.
The correct option is: Although the path was icy, nobody slipped.

golden: Although the path was icy, nobody slipped.
nearMiss: Although the path was icy nobody slipped.
Although, the path was icy nobody slipped.
Although the path was icy; nobody slipped.
_answerSpec.feedbackLong: 'Although the path was icy' is a subordinate adverbial clause showing concession. It needs a comma after it because it is fronted. A semicolon would wrongly treat it as an independent clause.
_answerSpec.answerText: Although the path was icy, nobody slipped.
_answerSpec.minimalHint: A subordinate clause usually depends on the main clause. Ask whether it can stand alone here.

items.promptText: Which option uses the correct tense, an appropriate modal verb, AND Standard English? A letter to parents about a school trip that will happen next week.
_solutionLines: This question tests tense aspect and modal verbs and standard english together.

The future tense ('will need') matches the upcoming trip. 'Should wear' is a suitable modal for advice. 'Should of' is non-standard (Standard English uses 'should have'), and past tense is wrong for a future event.
The correct option is: Your child will need a packed lunch and should wear comfortable shoes.

golden: Your child will need a packed lunch and should wear comfortable shoes.
nearMiss: Your child will need a packed lunch and should of wore comfortable shoes.
Your child needed a packed lunch and should wear comfortable shoes.
Your child will need a packed lunch and must of worn comfortable shoes.
_answerSpec.feedbackLong: The future tense ('will need') matches the upcoming trip. 'Should wear' is a suitable modal for advice. 'Should of' is non-standard (Standard English uses 'should have'), and past tense is wrong for a future event.
_answerSpec.answerText: Your child will need a packed lunch and should wear comfortable shoes.
_answerSpec.minimalHint: Choose the form you would expect in formal writing, not the spoken form you might hear.

items.promptText: Which option combines the correct past tense, a modal showing possibility, AND Standard English? A report about what happened during a science experiment yesterday.
_solutionLines: This question tests tense aspect and modal verbs and standard english together.
Past tense ('changed') matches yesterday. 'Could indicate' shows possibility. 'Could of' is non-standard. Present tense is wrong for a past event. 'Can' shows general ability, not past possibility.
The correct option is: The mixture changed colour, which could indicate a chemical reaction.

golden: The mixture changed colour, which could indicate a chemical reaction.
nearMiss: The mixture changed colour, which could of indicated a chemical reaction.
The mixture changes colour, which could indicate a chemical reaction.
The mixture changed colour, which can indicate a chemical reaction.
_answerSpec.feedbackLong: Past tense ('changed') matches yesterday. 'Could indicate' shows possibility. 'Could of' is non-standard. Present tense is wrong for a past event. 'Can' shows general ability, not past possibility.
_answerSpec.answerText: The mixture changed colour, which could indicate a chemical reaction.
_answerSpec.minimalHint: Check when the action happened and whether the sentence needs a simple, progressive, perfect, or past perfect form.

items.promptText: Which option uses the present perfect tense, a modal of certainty, AND Standard English? A notice about lost property that has been found.
_solutionLines: This question tests tense aspect and modal verbs and standard english together.
Present perfect ('has been found') shows the action is relevant now. 'Must belong' expresses certainty. 'Must of' is non-standard English; the correct form is 'must have'.
The correct option is: A blue coat has been found; it must belong to someone in Year 5.

golden: A blue coat has been found; it must belong to someone in Year 5.
nearMiss: A blue coat has been found; it must of belonged to someone in Year 5.
A blue coat was found; it must belong to someone in Year 5.
A blue coat has been found; it might of belonged to someone in Year 5.
_answerSpec.feedbackLong: Present perfect ('has been found') shows the action is relevant now. 'Must belong' expresses certainty. 'Must of' is non-standard English; the correct form is 'must have'.
_answerSpec.answerText: A blue coat has been found; it must belong to someone in Year 5.
_answerSpec.minimalHint: Choose the form you would expect in formal writing, not the spoken form you might hear.

items.promptText: Which option replaces the repeated noun with a pronoun AND maintains formal register? The council has approved the plans. The council will begin work in September.
_solutionLines: This question tests pronouns cohesion and formality together.
'It' avoids repetition of 'the council' (cohesion) while keeping the formal tone. 'They're gonna' is too informal. Repeating the noun weakens cohesion. 'Them lot' is non-standard.
The correct option is: The council has approved the plans. It will begin work in September.

nearMiss: The council has approved the plans. They're gonna start work in September.

_answerSpec.feedbackLong: 'It' avoids repetition of 'the council' (cohesion) while keeping the formal tone. 'They're gonna' is too informal. Repeating the noun weakens cohesion. 'Them lot' is non-standard.
_answerSpec.answerText: The council has approved the plans. It will begin work in September.
_answerSpec.minimalHint: A pronoun should make the sentence smoother without making the meaning unclear.

items.promptText: Which option improves cohesion AND keeps the writing formal? The museum opens at nine. The museum closes at five. Visitors must book online.

Joining the clauses avoids repeating 'The museum' (cohesion) and keeps formal vocabulary ('closes', 'must book'). 'Gotta' and 'You lot' are informal.

_answerSpec.feedbackLong: Joining the clauses avoids repeating 'The museum' (cohesion) and keeps formal vocabulary ('closes', 'must book'). 'Gotta' and 'You lot' are informal.
_answerSpec.answerText: The museum opens at nine and closes at five. Visitors must book online.
_answerSpec.minimalHint: Match the language to a formal setting, not everyday chat.
